---
title: "Gender Inequality Data Analysis"
subtitle: "Executive Summary"
author: "Jinwook Bae, Miriam Haf, Xuchen Shen, Andreas Sünder"
date: today
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
lang: en
fontsize: 10pt
linestretch: 0.95
format:
  pdf:
    papersize: a4
    geometry:
      - margin=1.5cm
header-includes:
  - \pagenumbering{gobble}
  - \RedeclareSectionCommand[beforeskip=.6\baselineskip,afterskip=.3\baselineskip]{section}
---

Gender inequality remains a prevalent issue in modern society, raising the question of the extent to which this inequality exists. This executive summary presents an overview of our team's data-driven analysis of biological gender inequality in education, care work, labor force participation, and the wage gap, and interprets their evolution over time across several countries.

Furthermore, we have analyzed correlations between related metrics to interpret results that supported or contradicted our overall conclusions. Our team has reached a reasonable conclusion using descriptive statistics and available data on this topic. 

## Problem and Methodology

Data was often missing for different countries in different years across all necessary datasets, causing issues of sparsity. To ensure meaningful conclusions, our analysis used multiple approaches to handle cross-sectional data.

Where possible, we selected the single most recent year with sufficient data coverage, as shown in the GII analysis on slide 4 or in Labor force participation rates on slide 16. When no single year provided sufficient coverage across countries, we defined a time window and extracted each country's most recent available data point within that range, maximizing the number of countries represented, as applied in the care-related labor inactivity analysis on slide 13.

When data sparsity was most severe, we first computed the median across all available data points for each country, then aggregated these country medians by region to derive a regional median. The resulting regional estimates are visualized by gender on slide 11 and shown as a gap on slide 21. To assess the reliability of country median data points with respect to the regional median, interquartile range error bars (Q1 to Q3) are displayed, indicating the dispersion of country-level medians within each region relative to the number of countries contributing to that estimate. 

Although this two-step approach did not reach concrete conclusions, it indicated general gender inequality, which was further analyzed for countries with sufficient data points later. When conducting cross-sectional analysis and portraying correlations between metrics, we only included countries with more than two data points over time. For our time series analysis among different countries, we extracted various countries in different regions that had sufficient data to be portrayed through time.

## Outcome and Outlook

The GII values on slide 4 partially contradicted the analysis of gender inequality fields. In the field of education, females and males tend to participate and graduate at different education levels almost equally in developed countries, with slight female dominance in higher education levels.

Other fields follow the pattern of the GII. Females spend more time on care work in all regions, and a bigger share of females is unemployed due to care responsibilities in comparison to males based on the latest data. As for Labor force participation, the same inequality applies for all selected countries. Furthermore, regardless of what education level one has achieved (except for tertiary education in specific countries), males seem to dominate in the labor market.

Finally, in the field of income, all but one continent show inequality in pay for females. Interestingly, when looking into specific countries, it shows that countries with lower GDP, hence, the countries with higher GII seem to show better pay for females, which suggests that economic growth in countries does not reflect back on their improvement of equal wage pay. This is portrayed by the weak correlation on slide 23, where GII values do not show a correlation with respect to the wage gap itself.

To take a deeper dive into this topic, looking into the size of inequality and economic shifts may give a coherent indication as to why inequality indices fluctuate amongst different regions. A GII to GDP correlation analysis may be feasible to track economic status with respect to gender inequality. Furthermore, our GII value formula takes health into account, which we have not looked into, but is open for further interpretation.
